title: Unnatural Love

jokes.text: Murray drives Bret and Jemaine to the nightclub but refuses to let them out despite their protests, insisting on taking them 'right to the door'
jokes.setup: Murray is driving the guys to a nightclub they don't want to attend
jokes.punchline: The more they resist, the more paternally insistent Murray becomes — treating grown adults like reluctant children
jokes.explanation: Classic Murray character comedy — his overbearing manager role extends to literal physical control over his band. The escalating 'keep going, keep going' as Bret and Jemaine beg to be let out is perfectly timed and completely in character. Sets the dynamic for the episode efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Last time I gave them $20 they went away and spent it. Bought a magazine and a giant beach ball.

jokes.text: Murray hands Dave $20 to babysit Bret and Jemaine, explaining 'Last time I gave them $20 they went away and spent it. Bought a magazine and a giant beach ball.'
jokes.setup: Murray doesn't trust Bret and Jemaine with money and arranges for Dave to supervise them
jokes.punchline: The reason: they previously spent $20 on 'a magazine and a giant beach ball' — the most charmingly useless, childlike purchases imaginable
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'a magazine and a giant beach ball' is what makes this exceptional. Not just a magazine — a GIANT beach ball. Two purchases that together paint a complete portrait of Bret and Jemaine's childlike uselessness. The economy is extraordinary: eight words that define two characters. Highly quotable and genuinely original in its specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Not cool enough.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You two?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, we're not that cool.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, everyone looks pretty cool.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: They're cooler than us.

jokes.text: Murray tells the bouncer he has 'a couple of very cool looking guys in the back of my car' — the camera presumably shows Bret and Jemaine looking profoundly uncool
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine worry they're not cool enough to get in
jokes.punchline: Murray describes them as 'very cool looking' to the bouncer — the gap between claim and reality is the joke
jokes.explanation: The irony works because we've just seen Bret and Jemaine nervously worrying about their coolness. Murray's cheerful, oblivious pitch to the bouncer — clearly failing — lands well. Not exceptional, but solidly constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I've got a couple of very cool looking guys in the back of my car. I don't know whether you're keen to have them in your club.

dialogue.speaker: Bouncer
dialogue.line: He's not coming in.

jokes.text: The nightclub DJ/host shouts 'Too many dicks on the dance floor!' and commands the men to spread out to 'create some lady space'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have just entered the nightclub
jokes.punchline: The DJ announces the club's problem with clinical matter-of-factness: there are simply too many dicks
jokes.explanation: The deadpan delivery of 'too many dicks' as a legitimate logistical nightclub problem is inspired. 'Dorking up my vibe with all the dicks' and 'create some lady space' are both extremely quotable. The transition into song is smooth. High originality — nobody had made this joke in song form before FotC.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Hey, guys, too many dicks.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Too many dicks.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What's that, man?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Too many. You guys are dorking up my vibe with all the dicks.

jokes.text: "You guys are dorking up my vibe with all the dicks" / "This isn't the riverdance anymore"
jokes.setup: MC is complaining about too many men crowded together on the dance floor
jokes.punchline: The Riverdance reference — comparing a male-heavy nightclub to a line-dancing troupe — is an unexpected and very specific image
jokes.explanation: The Riverdance simile is the most inspired line in the MC's preamble — unexpected, visual, and economical. 'Dorking up my vibe' is also a nice comic phrase.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This isn't the riverdance anymore.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ going to the party, sipping on bacardi / want to meet a hottie, but there's Adam, Steve and Marty ¶'
jokes.setup: Opening verse of 'Too Many Dicks' establishes the universal nightclub problem
jokes.punchline: The rhyme scheme turns a complaint into a comic poem — 'bacardi/hottie/Marty' chain delivers with precision
jokes.explanation: The switch from 'hottie' expectation to 'Adam, Steve and Marty' is a textbook misdirection executed musically. The rhyme scheme is tight. Billy, Todd, Tommy 'on leave from the army' extends the gag with specificity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: the only boobs I see tonight will be made of origami

jokes.text: Song: '¶ the only boobs I see tonight will be made of origami ¶'
jokes.setup: The song is cataloguing the absence of women at the party
jokes.punchline: Origami boobs — the most unexpected, specific, and absurd consolation prize imaginable
jokes.explanation: This is the standout lyric of the song and one of the episode's best jokes. 'Origami boobs' is breathtakingly specific and visual — someone at the party has folded paper into boob shapes as the only female representation available. It rhymes with 'army' perfectly. It's absurd, original, instantly quotable, and earns every laugh. Near-perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: it ain't no good if there's too much wood

jokes.text: Jemaine calls Bret from inside a girl's apartment: 'Bret, I think she might be Australian.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has gone home with a girl he met at the nightclub
jokes.punchline: His urgent middle-of-the-night call is not about danger or romance gone wrong — it's that she might be AUSTRALIAN, treated as an equally dire emergency
jokes.explanation: The entire New Zealand/Australia rivalry being played completely straight as a genuine romantic catastrophe is inspired. The tone of genuine distress over Australian nationality is perfect deadpan. Bret's immediate 'Oh, you've got to get out of there' confirms the shared absurd worldview. Beautifully character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: make sure you know before you go, the bro-to-ho ratio

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Either she's Australian or she really likes Australia.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks if Jemaine is sure she's Australian
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's hedge — she might just be a passionate Australia enthusiast — treats both possibilities as equally alarming
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — twelve words that reveal everything about Jemaine's panicked reasoning. The fact that 'really likes Australia' is treated as comparably troubling to actually being Australian is deeply funny and deeply in character. One of the episode's most quotable lines.

dialogue.line: five to one is a brodeo

jokes.text: Bret suggests Jemaine jump out the window; Jemaine agrees, then reports: 'Oh, it's too high.' Bret: 'Okay, well, do one of those dive rolls when you land.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine says he's 'locked in' by the Australian girl
jokes.punchline: Bret calmly suggests window escape and, when it's too high, upgrades to 'do a dive roll when you land' as if this is practical advice
jokes.explanation: The casual escalation from 'jump out the window' to 'do a dive roll' perfectly captures Bret and Jemaine's cheerfully incompetent problem-solving. The dive roll as genuinely offered tactical advice is absurd and character-true.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I need better odds, more broads, less rods

jokes.text: 'Oh, you've got to get out of there. Just get out of there.' / 'I'm locked in. She's trapped me.' / 'I'm not surprised.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is whispering from inside a girl's apartment as if in mortal danger
jokes.punchline: Bret's instant 'I'm not surprised' — delivered without elaboration — implies Jemaine is so hopeless that of course he'd be trapped
jokes.explanation: Bret's deadpan 'I'm not surprised' does a lot of work in three words. The whole sequence of treating an Australian girl's apartment like a hostage situation is strong character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I came to do battle, skedaddle with the cattle prods

jokes.text: Jemaine meets Keitha, discovers her name: 'Yeah, it's like Keith but with an 'a' at the end. I was named after me dad.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks Keitha her name — a routine social exchange
jokes.punchline: The name is a feminization of a man's name — and she was named after her dad, which deepens the joke perfectly
jokes.explanation: The name 'Keitha' is funny on its own as an absurd Australian naming convention, but adding 'I was named after me dad' gives it perfect structure — the setup is the name, the punchline is the inheritance. The beat of Jemaine's 'Pardon?... Keitha?' buys time for audience laughter.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, I think she might be Australian. Are you sure she's Australian? Either she's Australian or she really likes Australia.

jokes.text: Bret's voice from the phone: 'She's got a man's name.' Jemaine to Keitha: 'Keitha, that's a lovely name.'
jokes.setup: Bret is listening in via phone and has just heard the name
jokes.punchline: Bret's flat observation ('she's got a man's name') against Jemaine's simultaneous forced politeness ('that's a lovely name') creates a perfect split-screen comedy beat
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous commentary/performance split — Bret saying the true thing while Jemaine says the social thing — is a well-executed structural joke. The contrast works because both lines are delivered completely straight.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Jesus, got a tongue like a badger's asshole.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Jesus, got a tongue like a badger's asshole.'
jokes.setup: Keitha has kissed Jemaine while he's trying to escape
jokes.punchline: The comparison is vividly specific and deeply unflattering — yet delivered with the affect of someone reporting a weather condition
jokes.explanation: Exceptional specificity — not just 'rough' or 'bad,' but 'badger's asshole.' The precision of the insult delivered in the same breath as Jemaine is being kissed by her is brilliant timing. Highly quotable and very original. The deadpan delivery is what elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm not sure I got your name.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Keitha.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Pardon?

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Keitha.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Keitha?

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's like Keith but with an 'a' at the end. I was named after me dad.

jokes.text: After the kiss, Jemaine asks: 'Would you be able to unlock the door?' — his priority after intimate contact
jokes.setup: Keitha has just enthusiastically kissed Jemaine and is clearly interested
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's response is the most passionless possible: a polite request for exit
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's flat social politeness in the face of ardor is good character comedy. The formality of 'Would you be able to' is funny — it's how you ask a colleague to cc you on an email, not respond to a kiss.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's got a man's name.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Keitha, that's a lovely name.

jokes.text: Keitha, after unlocking the door to let Jemaine leave: 'Thanks for coming.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine has just escaped from Keitha's apartment
jokes.punchline: Keitha's cheerful, hostess-like farewell treats the encounter like a dinner party
jokes.explanation: Three words, completely deadpan, perfect double meaning. 'Thanks for coming' as a dismissive send-off after a one-night stand is brilliantly economical. Keitha's breezy obliviousness is established instantly.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Kind of like an evil version of our accent.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Does she sound Australian? Australian accent?' Jemaine: 'Yes. Yes.' Bret: 'What did it sound like?' Jemaine: 'Kind of like an evil version of our accent.'
jokes.setup: Bret is debriefing Jemaine on the extent of Keitha's Australianness
jokes.punchline: The Australian accent described as 'an evil version of our accent' — the NZ/Australia rivalry encoded as good vs. evil, completely straight-faced
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. The framing of Australian vs. New Zealand accents as evil vs. good — with complete sincerity — is perfect. It's character-specific (only a New Zealander would say this), observationally true (the accents ARE similar), and extraordinarily economical. Genuinely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Did you use protection?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, but only on my penis.

jokes.text: Bret: 'She may have subtly mocked your accent and you didn't notice.' Jemaine: 'She may have subtly been mocking me.'
jokes.setup: Bret is interrogating Jemaine about signs of Australian treachery
jokes.punchline: The solemn acceptance that Jemaine may have been subtly mocked without noticing — both men treat this as a genuine forensic concern
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy — the earnest paranoia about subtle mockery reveals insecurity played straight. Not the sharpest beat in the sequence but contributes to the running Australia anxiety gag.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's all red.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's lipstick.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's crabs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's not crabs.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: It's crabs.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's lipstick.

jokes.text: Bret: 'Did you use protection?' Jemaine: 'Yes, but only on my penis.'
jokes.setup: Bret asks the sensible safe-sex question
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's answer implies there are multiple areas requiring protection from an Australian, and he only secured one
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke construction. 'Yes, but only on my penis' simultaneously answers the question correctly AND opens up an entire paranoid universe where other body parts also needed protection from an Australian. The structure is flawless — setup reads as a normal question, punchline misdirects beautifully. One of the episode's strongest individual joke lines. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Purpose for the biscuits... you put 'NA.' What is 'NA'? Not applicable. There's no purpose for your biscuits?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I just wanted them.

jokes.text: Red mark on Jemaine's lip is identified as crabs by Bret. Jemaine insists it's lipstick. Bret: 'It's crabs.' Jemaine: 'It's not crabs.'
jokes.setup: Bret notices a red mark on Jemaine's lip
jokes.punchline: Bret's insistence that a visible lip smear is crabs — anatomically impossible but delivered as a sincere diagnosis
jokes.explanation: The running 'crabs' diagnosis works through Bret's unwavering conviction in the face of clear evidence to the contrary. The anatomy joke (crabs on a lip) is an implicit escalation of Australian-danger paranoia. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, they're hardly gonna send you biscuits if there's no purpose. Think about it.

jokes.text: 'She probably tried to steal your wallet.' / 'Don't touch anything.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine confirms he still has his wallet
jokes.punchline: Bret still assumes she tried and failed, then warns him not to touch anything — treating Australian contact like a biohazard
jokes.explanation: 'Don't touch anything' — treating the aftermath of sleeping with an Australian like a crime scene — is a good deadpan button on the sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine slept with an Australian.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine slept with an Australian.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, I heard you. I said 'what' as in I heard you but I can't believe what I'm hearing.

jokes.text: Murray's meeting agenda item: government-approved biscuits. Bret's form was approved; Jemaine's was rejected because he wrote 'NA' for purpose of biscuits.
jokes.setup: Murray runs band meetings like formal management sessions
jokes.punchline: The formality of a government biscuit approval process — and Jemaine's rejection for not explaining why he wanted biscuits ('I just wanted them') — is peak bureaucratic absurdism
jokes.explanation: Perfectly calibrated absurdism. The New Zealand government bureaucracy approving BISCUITS for band members, with a formal application form, is the kind of deeply weird worldbuilding FotC excels at. Murray's earnest 'well, they're hardly gonna send you biscuits if there's no purpose. Think about it.' is hilarious in its reasonableness within the absurd framework.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: How do you accidently sleep with one? What, did she get you naked and you tripped over and fell on her?

jokes.text: Murray asks if Jemaine can have one of Bret's biscuits 'until I get this sorted' — the camera holds on Bret's reluctant reaction before he relents
jokes.setup: Jemaine's biscuit form was rejected; Murray asks Bret to share
jokes.punchline: The pause before Bret gives Jemaine a biscuit — his internal conflict over sharing government-approved biscuits — is a small but perfect reaction beat
jokes.explanation: Murray's 'That's the boy. That's good. Nice.' as Bret relents — treating the sharing of a single biscuit like a significant diplomatic achievement — extends the absurdism effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jemaine's a good-looking guy, isn't he? There he goes. Yeah, he's listening.

jokes.text: Murray transitions meeting: 'Right, let's move on. Next, nightclubbing experiences. Anything to report back?'
jokes.setup: After handling biscuit bureaucracy, Murray continues the meeting
jokes.punchline: 'Nightclubbing experiences' as a formal agenda item, solicited like a business report, is absurd meeting-management comedy
jokes.explanation: Murray's managerial framing of social activities as business deliverables is consistent character comedy. Less sharp here but serves the scene's rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You know, guys, in the old days the sailors used to fall for them. Yeah, they hadn't seen a woman for weeks and the australians would lure them to the bottom of the sea and they'd drown.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh, that's mermaids. That's mermaids you're thinking of.

jokes.text: Bret reveals Jemaine's secret: 'Jemaine slept with an Australian.' Murray: 'What?!' — then clarifies he said 'what' as disbelief not mishearing
jokes.setup: Bret blurts out Jemaine's secret in the meeting
jokes.punchline: Murray's clarification — 'I said what as in I heard you but I can't believe what I'm hearing' — the over-explanation of a simple emotional reaction is perfectly Murray
jokes.explanation: Murray's literal-minded explanation of his emotional reaction is perfect character work. The 'what' disambiguation is a genuinely original structural beat — he anticipated being misunderstood and pre-corrects.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, but the Australian ones were the worst. That's what I'm trying to say.

jokes.text: Murray: 'How do you accidently sleep with one? What, did she get you naked and you tripped over and fell on her? Is that what happened?'
jokes.setup: Jemaine says he 'accidentally' slept with an Australian
jokes.punchline: Murray's literal interpretation of 'accidentally' — picturing a physical tumble — is gloriously specific and deadpan
jokes.explanation: Murray's literalism is in full force. 'Did she get you naked and you tripped over and fell on her' is a specific, vivid, and absurd physical scenario that works perfectly as a comedic interpretation. Very Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Thanks. It's me mom's recipe. Oh, is your mom Australian?

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Yeah yeah, she's a panel beater.

jokes.text: Murray whispers to Bret with Jemaine turned away: 'Do you think he's listening? Let's test it. Jemaine's a good-looking guy, isn't he?' — Jemaine immediately turns around
jokes.setup: Murray tells Jemaine not to listen while he talks to Bret
jokes.punchline: Murray's test — complimenting Jemaine to provoke a reaction — immediately proves Jemaine was listening; Murray: 'Yeah, he's listening.'
jokes.explanation: A neat, well-structured physical comedy beat. Murray's choice to test with a compliment rather than an insult is a nice wrinkle — Jemaine can't resist hearing himself praised. Plays as a quick, satisfying mini-gag.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Yeah yeah, me dad's so Australian he's in prison.

jokes.text: Murray devises a test for whether Jemaine is listening: 'Jemaine's a good-looking guy, isn't he?' — Jemaine visibly reacts — 'Yeah, he's listening.'
jokes.setup: Murray wants to discuss Jemaine's fate secretly while Jemaine sits nearby
jokes.punchline: Murray tests whether Jemaine is listening by saying something flattering about him — Jemaine immediately turns, confirming he's eavesdropping
jokes.explanation: Clever and well-constructed. The idea of using a compliment as an eavesdropping test is genuinely original, and Jemaine's immediate reaction to hearing himself praised is perfectly played. The structure (setup test → confirm failure → move on) is tight. One of the better comedic constructions in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: My great-great-grandpa was a renown rapist. And they shipped him out to Australia and that's where he met my great-great-grandma. She was a prostitute. I mean, I said met, but he raped her.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Maybe we should banish him, you know, from the group. Cast him off. Never speak of him again, just for a couple of days.'
jokes.setup: Murray is considering disciplinary action for Jemaine sleeping with an Australian
jokes.punchline: 'Just for a couple of days' — the dramatic escalation to banishment immediately undercut by the casual time limit
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation/undercut structure. 'Banish him, cast him off, never speak of him again' builds to maximum drama — then 'just for a couple of days' collapses it entirely. The specificity of 'a couple of days' for what sounded like eternal exile is very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Sit around here asking me stupid questions, get in that bedroom and root me again.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: They're both good options.

jokes.text: Murray explains Australian danger using mermaid mythology: 'The sailors used to fall for them... the Australians would lure them to the bottom of the sea and they'd drown.' Bret: 'Oh, that's mermaids.'
jokes.setup: Murray warns about Australian women with historical precedent
jokes.punchline: Murray has confused Australians with mermaids; when corrected, he insists: 'Yeah, but the Australian ones were the worst'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. Murray's sincere conflation of Australians and mermaids — and his refusal to fully concede the point even when corrected ('Yeah, but the Australian ones were the worst') — is deeply funny. The logic is perfectly Murray: yes, okay, those are mermaids, but still, the Australian ones were dangerous. Wonderful absurdist character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: do australians feel love? are they capable of love? do they even know what we're speaking of?

jokes.text: Jemaine goes to Keitha to retrieve his wallet — she has it. He came back to the Australian's place after all that panic.
jokes.setup: The entire meeting was about the horror of Jemaine's Australian encounter — Murray warned him, they discussed his safety
jokes.punchline: Jemaine shows up at Keitha's door immediately after — his wallet as a thin pretext — and she casually invites him in for a cuppa
jokes.explanation: Efficient structural irony — the cut from Murray's warnings straight to Jemaine at her door works as a visual callback. 'Would you like to come in for a cuppa' and Jemaine clearly wavering despite himself is good character truth.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That was a misunderstanding. You were wearing a vest top.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: A my mom gave me that. She thought it made me look like bruce willis.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, it didn't. It made you look like an Australian.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'Oh, come on, you big mung.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is hesitating about coming back inside Keitha's flat
jokes.punchline: 'You big mung' — a quintessentially Australian/larrikin term of exasperated endearment that lands as both insult and affection
jokes.explanation: Keitha's dialect and casual abrasiveness are consistently funny. 'You big mung' is an authentically weird Australian-ism that works as character establishment.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've got a real aussie accent. Except it's not as strong as it used to be since I've lived here, 'cause every time I'm on the phone with my mom she says I sound like marilyn monroe.

jokes.text: Keitha explains her dad is 'so Australian he's in prison' and her great-great-grandfather was 'a renowned rapist' shipped to Australia
jokes.setup: Jemaine is interrogating the depth of Keitha's Australian heritage
jokes.punchline: The Australian convict-history joke taken to maximum literal extent — her family's Australianness literally traced to a convicted rapist transported to the colony
jokes.explanation: The Australian convict heritage joke is an obvious target but FotC commits to it fully — the specificity of 'renowned rapist' (who then raped her great-great-grandmother) is dark enough to land as subversive. Keitha's matter-of-fact delivery ('I mean I said met, but he raped her') is the key.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, I suppose if you squint your ears.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'You got two options. Sit around here asking me stupid questions, [or] get in that bedroom and root me again.' Jemaine: 'They're both good options.'
jokes.setup: Keitha gives Jemaine an ultimatum after his questions about her Australian heritage
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's assessment that both the 'stupid questions' option AND sex are equally good options — his mild delivery making it funny
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's deadpan assessment that 'they're both good options' treats the stupid-questions option as equally appealing as sex, which is a good character beat. 'Root me again' is also a great Australian-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Got to murder a Brown snake, you know

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Do Australians feel love? / Are they capable of love? / Do they even know what we're speaking of? ¶'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is developing feelings for Keitha despite everything
jokes.punchline: The existential question 'do Australians feel love' delivered as a sincere, earnest folk song — the anti-Australian prejudice expressed through beautiful, plaintive music
jokes.explanation: The song title and hook is perfect FotC — treating provincial prejudice with the gravitas of a love ballad. 'Do Australians feel love? Are they capable of love?' sung with complete sincerity maps perfectly onto the racist-love-song tradition while undercutting it brilliantly. High character integration — only Jemaine would sing this.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: And your children, what about them? What would become of them? They'll be aberrations, won't they?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It's pronounced aborigines.

jokes.text: Jemaine wakes Bret in the night to ask a 'hypothetical question,' then says 'I suppose so' when Bret asks if it can wait till morning — and immediately wakes him again
jokes.setup: Jemaine wakes Bret urgently in the night
jokes.punchline: The question can apparently wait — but Jemaine wakes him AGAIN almost immediately with the same urgency
jokes.explanation: The timing between the 'I suppose so' and the second waking is the joke — Jemaine literally cannot wait. A reliable comedic structure (false resolution immediately undone) executed cleanly.

dialogue.line: forced to move from city to city looking for the perfect wave.

jokes.text: Bret: 'When I first met you, you tried to have me deported from New Zealand because you thought I was Australian. That was a misunderstanding. You were wearing a vest top.' 'My mom gave me that. She thought it made me look like Bruce Willis.'
jokes.setup: Bret warns Jemaine that he'd never go out with an Australian, citing their history
jokes.punchline: Bret was nearly deported for wearing a vest top (tank top) — which apparently reads as Australian; his mom thought it made him look like Bruce Willis
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific backstory — the vest top nearly causing deportation from NZ because it read as Australian is absurd on multiple levels. The Bruce Willis detail is perfect misdirection. 'Well, it didn't. It made you look like an Australian.' is the perfect landing.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: See you later, big j.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Let's go, little b.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Okay, little m.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: Medium m.

jokes.text: 'What would you think if I did go out with that... Australian?' / 'I would never go out with an Australian.' / 'If you were to, I would be fine with it.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine asks a 'hypothetical' — would Bret be fine with him dating an Australian?
jokes.punchline: Bret immediately says he'd never do it himself, then claims he'd be fine with it for Jemaine — a perfect little moment of hypocrisy and misdirected judgment
jokes.explanation: Bret's double standard — 'I would never, but for you I'd be fine with it' — is a good character beat. The prejudice-as-relationship-advice structure parodies similar conversations about interracial dating.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Uh, it's you putting on a woman's voice pretending to be keitha breaking up with me. It's clearly you, Bret. I don't think so. It's obviously you putting on a woman's voice.

jokes.text: 'My mom gave me that. She thought it made me look like Bruce Willis.' / 'Well, it didn't. It made you look like an Australian.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine defends the vest top his mom gave him, saying she thought he looked like Bruce Willis
jokes.punchline: Bret corrects him: not Bruce Willis, Australian
jokes.explanation: Excellent capping joke. Bruce Willis as the maternal aspiration for a son's fashion, contrasted with the actual result (looking Australian), is a perfect comedy beat. The specificity of Bruce Willis makes it — not 'manly' or 'tough,' specifically Bruce Willis. Deadpan delivery makes both lines land.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's not from wollongong.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Shh.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She's from wollamaloo.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Hey, she'd never be my girlfriend.' — cut to Jemaine introducing Keitha as his girlfriend
jokes.setup: Jemaine insists to Bret that Keitha would never be his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Immediate cut to Jemaine introducing her as exactly that
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom ironic cut — the 'I would never' immediately followed by exactly that. Well-executed even if the structure is familiar. The visual of Jemaine standing there with Keitha makes it land.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine, did I show you my gloves that look like my hands?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Get off. Get off. Let go of my glove.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ow, that's actually my hand.

jokes.text: Murray: 'What is that you're wearing?' Bret: 'Where'd you get that?' — reaction to whatever Jemaine is wearing (implied Australian-influenced outfit)
jokes.setup: Jemaine arrives with Keitha as his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Both Murray and Bret immediately clock what Jemaine is wearing before anything else — their horror at his clothing choice (presumably Australian-influenced) takes precedence over meeting the girlfriend
jokes.explanation: Visual gag dependent on what Jemaine is wearing — the reaction is broad and clear from dialogue. The prioritization of outfit horror over girlfriend-meeting is a small but effective beat.

dialogue.line: She doesn't need a sweatshirt in Australia. It's hot.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'You can call me Keith, by the way.' Murray: 'That's a man's name.' Keitha: 'It's a female name. It's got an 'a' on the end.'
jokes.setup: Keitha offers a shortened version of her name
jokes.punchline: Murray's blunt 'that's a man's name' echoes Bret's earlier reaction — but Keitha's defense ('it's got an 'a' on the end') is perfect deadpan logic
jokes.explanation: The running gag of everyone reacting to the name pays off again with Murray. Keitha's unwavering confidence in 'it's a female name, it's got an a on the end' is good deadpan.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: No, mostly it's 'cause they think you're a dick. You know, with the dickish glasses and that.

jokes.text: Murray: 'Got quite the accent, don't you, Kevina?' — mispronouncing Keitha's name
jokes.setup: Murray has just been told her name is Keitha
jokes.punchline: He immediately calls her 'Kevina' — a completely different wrong name — presumably mishearing or misremembering
jokes.explanation: Murray's oblivious mispronunciation is a lovely detail — he's immediately established Keitha as 'Kevina' in his head and moved on. Very Murray in its combination of authority and complete wrongness.

dialogue.speaker: Keitha
dialogue.line: No, mostly it's 'cause they think I've rooted better-looking fellas when I've been too drunk to speak.

jokes.text: Keitha says she sounds like Marilyn Monroe on the phone to her mom. Murray and Bret's reactions: 'Did you catch that?' / 'What are you deaf?' as they struggle to process the claim
jokes.setup: Keitha claims her accent has softened since living in New York and she now sounds like Marilyn Monroe
jokes.punchline: Murray and Bret's total failure to immediately comprehend this information — mutual bewilderment — and Bret's 'I suppose if you squint your ears'
jokes.explanation: 'If you squint your ears' is the standout line here — a brilliantly original mixed metaphor that captures the gap between what Keitha claims and what everyone else hears. The confused processing by Murray and Bret adds to the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: felicity saw there was no electricity, Emily, no chemistry, fran ran, turned out to be a man

jokes.text: Murray: 'And your children, what about them? What would become of them? They'll be aberrations, won't they?' Jemaine: 'It's pronounced aborigines.'
jokes.setup: Murray is listing reasons why Jemaine can't date an Australian
jokes.punchline: Murray uses 'aberrations' to mean mixed-race children; Jemaine corrects him as though he meant 'aborigines' — and this correction is both a malapropism joke AND reveals what Murray might actually have meant
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best single joke. The construction is immaculate: Murray says 'aberrations,' intending it to mean something monstrous; Jemaine corrects 'it's pronounced aborigines' — which (a) is funny as a simple malapropism correction, (b) suggests Jemaine thinks Murray meant something specific and more offensive, (c) reveals that Murray MIGHT have meant aborigines, and (d) the correction itself is gentle and matter-of-fact. Multiple layers firing simultaneously. Perfect economy, perfect character integration, perfectly structured. Genuinely exceptional joke-writing.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: carol Brown just took a bus out of town, but I'm hoping that you'll stick around

jokes.text: Murray: 'Think of them. They'll be neither here nor there, forced to move from city to city looking for the perfect wave.'
jokes.setup: Murray is warning about the fate of Jemaine and Keitha's hypothetical children
jokes.punchline: The children's tragic destiny: searching for 'the perfect wave' — a surf metaphor for existential Australian-New Zealander rootlessness
jokes.explanation: Murray's absurd image of doomed half-Australian children eternally surfing is a lovely flight of character-specific reasoning. The 'perfect wave' lands as an unexpectedly lyrical and absurd conclusion to his argument.

dialogue.line: he doesn't cook or clean, he's not good boyfriend material, oo-whee and eats cereal

jokes.text: Keitha excuses herself: 'I'm gonna go to the dunny. Got to murder a Brown snake, you know.'
jokes.setup: Keitha excuses herself from the group
jokes.punchline: The Australian slang for bathroom ('dunny') plus 'murder a brown snake' — a vivid Australianism for defecating, delivered completely naturally to horrified New Zealanders
jokes.explanation: Keitha's casual Australian idioms are consistently funny because of how naturally she delivers them vs. the group's horrified reactions. 'Murder a brown snake' is a spectacularly gross-out expression delivered as small talk. Murray's 'oh my God' is the perfect reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: who organized all of my ex-girlfriends into a choir and got them to sing?

jokes.text: 'She can't come to Christmas.' / 'She's gonna ridicule us at Christmas.' / 'Maybe I will go to her house for Christmas.' / 'I don't have to do everything you say.'
jokes.setup: Murray insists Keitha can't attend Christmas
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's quiet rebellion — 'maybe I will go to her house' — treated as a shocking act of defiance; then 'I don't have to do everything you say' as the first hint of independence
jokes.explanation: Good character development joke — Jemaine asserting minor independence. 'She's gonna ridicule us at Christmas' suggests Murray's primary fear is mockery from an Australian.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: tiffany, you said that you had an epiphany

jokes.text: Murray to Jemaine: 'And sit up straight. You're slouching terribly.' / 'No, don't stand up, just sit up.'
jokes.setup: In the middle of an intense argument about Jemaine's romantic future
jokes.punchline: Murray interrupts with a posture correction — completely out of proportion to the emotional stakes of the conversation
jokes.explanation: Murray's shift from existential dread about Jemaine's children to 'sit up straight' is perfect character comedy — his manager/parent mode overrides even the gravest topics. 'No, don't stand up, just sit up' is a precise specification that only Murray would make.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: mmm, would you like a little cereal?

jokes.text: Murray: 'She's probably in the toilet thinking of something to bring me down with.'
jokes.setup: Keitha has excused herself to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Murray's paranoid Australian-conspiracy theory: she's in there strategizing mockery
jokes.explanation: Murray's anti-Australian paranoia reaching into her bathroom break is a good extension of the episode's running gag. The specificity of 'something to bring me down with' is good Murray-speak.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait a second. Which one are you?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: This is Bret.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Bret.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Yeah, not looking for me.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'See you later, big j.' Jemaine: 'Let's go, little b.' Bret: 'Okay, little m.' Murray: 'Medium m.'
jokes.setup: The group is leaving Keitha after the meeting
jokes.punchline: Murray's self-correction to 'medium m' — not satisfied with 'little' for himself — is a tiny beat of vanity that's perfectly Murray
jokes.explanation: The chain of abbreviated nicknames is charming, but Murray's self-important correction from 'little' to 'medium' is the perfect punchline. It reveals Murray's self-image — not little, not large, but medium — in two words. Exceptional character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I just think it's really cool that you love each other even though you're from austria and you're from someplace no one's even fucking heard of.

jokes.text: Jemaine makes a show of noticing an answer phone message 'for you on the answer phone' — clearly having listened to it already
jokes.setup: Jemaine tells Bret about a message on the answer phone
jokes.punchline: Bret reveals he knows because it's obviously him doing a fake Australian voice pretending to be Keitha
jokes.explanation: The buildup to the fake-voicemail reveal is nicely handled — Jemaine's overly casual 'I think there may be a message for you' tells us he already knows.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's like that movie, 'interracial hole stretchers 2.' She was white and they were black, but it didn't matter in the end, did it? Because they were in love.

jokes.text: The 'Keitha' voicemail: clearly Bret doing a bad Australian accent, getting geographic details wrong ('Wollongong' vs. 'Woollamaloo'), ending with '...And I'm married.' then a man's voice: 'Who's on the phone, love?'
jokes.setup: Bret has left a fake voicemail from 'Keitha' breaking up with Jemaine
jokes.punchline: The message escalates: she's moving to the wrong city, never seeing him again — then 'And I'm married,' capped by her husband's voice
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed escalating reveal. The voicemail is funny as a fake from the start (wrong city, obviously Bret's voice), then 'And I'm married' lands as a genuine shock twist — and the husband's voice asking 'who's on the phone, love?' suggests this might actually be REAL. The structure is excellent: comedy of fakeness suddenly becoming potentially true.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How much is it to new Jersey, by the way?

dialogue.speaker: Carriage driver
dialogue.line: I don't go to new Jersey. I only ride around the park.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's weird.

dialogue.speaker: Carriage driver
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She said she'd done it before.

jokes.text: Jemaine tries to leave after the voicemail disaster. Bret: 'Jemaine, did I show you my gloves that look like my hands?' Jemaine: 'Get off. Let go of my glove. Ow, that's actually my hand.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is upset and trying to leave
jokes.punchline: Bret tries to distract him with gloves that look like hands; in the struggle Bret grabs Jemaine's actual hand instead of the glove
jokes.explanation: The gloves-that-look-like-hands is a wonderfully absurd detail — Bret apparently owns these. The physical confusion of grabbing Jemaine's actual hand is a solid slapstick beat. 'That's actually my hand' delivered in pain is a funny pay-off.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Bret, have you seen keitha? She robbed us. Her and her friends jumped me and taped me to the door.

jokes.text: 'Get off. Get off. Let go of my glove. Ow, that's actually my hand. That's actually my hand.'
jokes.setup: Bret is holding Jemaine's glove/hand to stop him leaving
jokes.punchline: Jemaine realizes Bret is squeezing his actual hand, not the glove — extending the absurd glove joke into physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical extension of the glove joke — the confusion between glove and actual hand is exactly the right payoff for 'gloves that look like my hands.' The repetition of 'that's actually my hand' adds to the escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Did she mention me at all?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, sorry, man.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I'm not sure about her and me.

jokes.text: At Keitha's building, the roommate denies Keitha is there ('she's moved back to Australia'). During this clearly false denial, Jemaine can see Keitha in the background.
jokes.setup: Jemaine arrives to find Keitha; roommate insists she's gone back to Australia
jokes.punchline: Keitha is visibly present the entire time — 'She doesn't need a sweatshirt in Australia. It's hot' while Jemaine can clearly see her
jokes.explanation: Classic visible-lie visual gag — reliable structure, executed competently. The sweatshirt excuse ('she doesn't need one in Australia, it's hot') is a nice detail.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Can I get a hug? Huh? Can I get a hug? Hug?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No. No. A hug? Unfair. I'm not participating in this hug.

jokes.text: 'I don't believe you. That's her sweatshirt.' / 'She doesn't need a sweatshirt in Australia. It's hot.'
jokes.setup: The flatmates claim Keitha has returned to Australia; Jemaine points to her sweatshirt as evidence she's still there
jokes.punchline: 'She doesn't need a sweatshirt in Australia. It's hot.' — a completely reasonable but somehow hilarious justification for why a sweatshirt proves nothing
jokes.explanation: 'She doesn't need a sweatshirt in Australia. It's hot.' — delivered deadpan — is a perfectly logical but absurd response. The flatmates are clearly bad liars and this makes it funnier. Good economy.

jokes.text: After the roommates insist Keitha is gone ('she's in Australia like we said'), Keitha walks right past and says 'Hey, big j.'
jokes.setup: Roommates have been insisting Keitha is in Australia
jokes.punchline: Keitha walks past anyway — the cover story collapses immediately with zero effort from Jemaine
jokes.explanation: The roommates' cover collapsing the moment Keitha casually strolls by is satisfying payoff — she doesn't even try to hide. Her cheerful 'Hey, big j' followed by 'Bye' as she continues walking is perfectly Keitha.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'No, mostly it's 'cause they think you're a dick. You know, with the dickish glasses and that.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine tells Keitha their friends think their love can never be because of nationality differences
jokes.punchline: Keitha reframes it: her friends just think he's a dick — specifically because of his glasses
jokes.explanation: Keitha's reframing — cutting through Jemaine's romantic framing with blunt assessment — is sharp. 'Dickish glasses' is a memorable, very specific insult. The deflation of Jemaine's romantic narrative is efficiently brutal. 'And that' is a perfect casual qualifier.

jokes.text: Keitha: 'No, mostly it's 'cause they think I've rooted better-looking fellas when I've been too drunk to speak.' Jemaine: 'Is that how you feel?' Keitha: '50-50. I kinda like you.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine continues to frame their relationship as a star-crossed romance
jokes.punchline: Keitha's friends think she's lowering her standards; her honest assessment is '50-50' — the most deflating possible romantic evaluation
jokes.explanation: '50-50. I kinda like you.' as the romantic declaration after 'I've rooted better-looking fellas' is perfectly calibrated anti-romance. Keitha's complete lack of sentimentality is character-consistent and genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Song 'Carol Brown': '¶ Loretta broke my heart in a letter / Joan broke it off over the phone / after the tone she left me alone ¶'
jokes.setup: Jemaine sings about his history of breakups
jokes.punchline: Joan broke up via voicemail specifically 'after the tone' — the hold music / answerphone tone as a punchline mid-verse
jokes.explanation: 'After the tone she left me alone' works on two levels — the rhyme and the literal description of leaving a voicemail breakup. Compact and clever within the song's structure.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Liza got amnesia, just forgot who I am ¶'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is cataloguing how his exes left him
jokes.punchline: Liza's 'amnesia' — the implication that forgetting him entirely was her excuse/breakup method — delivered with complete sad sincerity
jokes.explanation: The amnesia line is delivered with complete self-pity — the implication that someone faked amnesia to avoid breaking up with him is dark and funny. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Felicity saw there was no electricity / Emily, no chemistry / Fran ran, turned out to be a man ¶'
jokes.setup: The song continues cataloguing departing exes
jokes.punchline: The escalating rhyme scheme builds to 'Fran ran, turned out to be a man' — the revelation that one 'ex-girlfriend' was actually a man is the escalation peak of this verse
jokes.explanation: The electricity/chemistry setup is already clever, but 'Fran ran, turned out to be a man' is a perfect escalation — rhyming, rhythmically tight, and a genuine surprise. It's one of the song's funniest moments. The compressed revelation ('turned out to be a man') delivered as a matter-of-fact fourth item in a list is inspired.

jokes.text: Song: Jemaine's ex-girlfriends appear AS A CHOIR to sing criticisms of him ('He doesn't cook or clean / he's not good boyfriend material / oo-whee and eats cereal')
jokes.setup: Jemaine's romantic song about lost loves
jokes.punchline: His exes materialize as a literal backing choir to undermine the song with their own commentary on why he's a bad boyfriend
jokes.explanation: The ex-girlfriend choir is a brilliant formal conceit — taking the usual 'backing singers' trope and making the backing singers the antagonists of the song. The complaints are specific ('eats cereal' as a key failing) and delivered with gospel sincerity. This is the song's central comedic device and it's genuinely original and impressive as musical comedy writing.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Oh, who organized all of my ex-girlfriends into a choir and got them to sing? / Who? Who? ¶'
jokes.setup: The ex-girlfriend choir has been singing criticisms of Jemaine
jokes.punchline: Jemaine breaks the fourth wall of the song to demand to know who organized this — the absurdist logic becomes self-aware
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. The meta-question — Jemaine genuinely demanding to know who organized his ex-girlfriends into a choir — is funny on multiple levels: (1) it acknowledges the absurdity from within the absurdity, (2) it treats the practical logistics of ex-girlfriend choir organizing as a legitimate grievance, (3) the repeated 'Who? Who?' has a desperate quality that's deeply funny. The genius is that Jemaine is distressed by the organizational aspect, not the fact of a choir. Comparable to the very best moments in FotC's musical comedy.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Mmm, shut up ¶' — Jemaine's response to the choir's criticisms
jokes.setup: The choir has been extensively cataloguing Jemaine's failings as a partner
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's entire counter-argument to a full choir of evidence is 'shut up' — delivered with gentle soulfulness
jokes.explanation: Peak economy. The gospel-inflected 'Mmm, shut up' — delivered with full musical sincerity against a choir of ex-girlfriend evidence — is a tiny masterpiece. The 'Mmm' before 'shut up' gives it musical weight. Jemaine's only rebuttal is the most emotionally accurate, least eloquent possible response. Genuinely exceptional.

jokes.text: Song: The ex-girlfriend choir debates whether Jemaine's romantic talk is sincere. Choir: '¶ He means he looks at other girls ¶' / '¶ This guy is a fool / He'll always be a boy / He's a man who never grew up ¶' Jemaine: '¶ I thought I told you to shut up ¶'
jokes.setup: The choir is analyzing Jemaine's romantic declarations in real-time
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'I thought I told you to shut up' — calling back the earlier 'shut up' but now with exasperated authority, as if one 'shut up' should have been enough
jokes.explanation: The callback to 'shut up' now delivered with wounded authority — as if Jemaine genuinely expected one 'shut up' to silence a choir of ex-girlfriends — is excellent. The choir's real-time analysis of his romantic line ('he means he looks at other girls') is sharp too.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Lola, you told me you were in a coma ¶'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is addressing specific exes directly
jokes.punchline: Lola apparently claimed to be in a coma to avoid him — a significantly more dramatic excuse than amnesia, delivered with equal resigned sadness
jokes.explanation: The coma excuse is a wonderful escalation from Liza's amnesia earlier — Jemaine's exes are apparently willing to fake medical emergencies to avoid him. Delivered with complete sincerity and self-pity.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Tiffany, you said that you had an epiphany ¶'
jokes.setup: Jemaine continues addressing exes
jokes.punchline: The rhyme of 'Tiffany/epiphany' is itself the joke — the word 'epiphany' chosen purely for rhyme, suggesting Tiffany's excuse was appropriately grandiose
jokes.explanation: The rhyme is technically impressive and the concept is funny — an 'epiphany' as a breakup excuse is pompous. Good economy.

jokes.text: Song: '¶ Mmm, would you like a little cereal? ¶' — Jemaine offers Keitha cereal mid-song
jokes.setup: Jemaine is mid-romantic-song addressed to Keitha
jokes.punchline: He breaks from singing to offer her cereal — the choir's complaint that 'he eats cereal' becoming literally true mid-song; Keitha is present and this is a real offer
jokes.explanation: Exceptional callback and structural payoff. The choir complained 'he eats cereal' as a relationship failing — now, in the middle of his romantic ballad to Keitha, Jemaine literally offers her cereal. It proves the choir right in real time, it breaks the song's emotional register perfectly, and it's an extraordinarily specific and original joke. The delivery — sung, in the middle of a sincere musical plea — makes it land as one of the episode's top moments. Near-perfect on all craft dimensions.

jokes.text: Dave, after being asked about Jemaine: 'Wait a second. Which one are you?' — to Bret, who is standing right in front of him
jokes.setup: Murray and Bret approach Dave to ask if he's seen Jemaine
jokes.punchline: Dave can't distinguish Bret from Jemaine despite knowing them both — a running gag about Dave's obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Dave's obliviousness about which band member is which is a running show gag. Functional but not especially sharp — the joke relies more on recognition of Dave as a character type than on fresh comedy.

jokes.text: Dave farewell: 'See you, Murray. See you, Jemaine.' (misidentifying Bret as Jemaine). Then he's gone before they can correct him.
jokes.setup: Dave has been told he's talking to Bret, not Jemaine
jokes.punchline: He leaves calling Bret 'Jemaine' anyway — the information hasn't registered at all
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the previous beat — Dave's farewell confirming he retained nothing from the name correction. Reliable running gag payoff, but relatively mechanical.

jokes.text: Dave: 'I just think it's really cool that you love each other even though you're from Austria and you're from someplace no one's even fucking heard of.' Murray: 'Australia.' Bret: 'New Zealand.'
jokes.setup: Dave offers an emotional tribute to cross-cultural love
jokes.punchline: Dave misidentifies both countries — calling Australia 'Austria' and New Zealand 'someplace no one's even fucking heard of'
jokes.explanation: 'Someplace no one's even fucking heard of' is a gut-punch to New Zealand's cultural invisibility — delivered with sincere affection by Dave, who means well. The Austria/Australia mix-up is a classic American-obliviousness joke but the New Zealand line is sharper.

jokes.text: Dave: 'It's like that movie, Interracial Hole Stretchers 2. She was white and they were black, but it didn't matter in the end, did it? Because they were in love. I haven't seen that one. Well, it really affected me.'
jokes.setup: Dave is drawing an inspirational parallel to film about cross-cultural love
jokes.punchline: The film is clearly pornography — 'Interracial Hole Stretchers 2'; Dave's description of it as a love story, and his admission of being deeply moved by it, is the joke
jokes.explanation: Dave citing pornography as an emotional touchstone for his philosophy on love is perfectly in character — and the added detail that 'it really affected me' elevates it from a simple shock joke to genuine character comedy. Murray and Bret's non-response ('I haven't seen that one') is pitch-perfect deadpan. One of Dave's best moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Jemaine reveals his elopement destination: New Jersey. 'Wow. New Jersey, huh? I'd love to go there.' Dave: 'But I got a lot of shit going on in the states right now.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine announces he and Keitha are eloping
jokes.punchline: New Jersey as a romantic elopement destination; Dave's casual regret that he can't go — 'got a lot of shit going on in the states right now'
jokes.explanation: Dave's 'I got a lot of shit going on in the states right now' — describing not being able to go to New Jersey from New York City — is excellent deadpan. He's already in 'the states.' The New Jersey elopement as a grand romantic gesture is also a good understatement.

jokes.text: Dave to Jemaine: 'Don't drink the water.' — as Jemaine prepares to elope to New Jersey
jokes.setup: Jemaine is leaving for New Jersey
jokes.punchline: Dave's ominous parting advice about New Jersey water — both a real concern and an absurd final note
jokes.explanation: Three words, delivered with completely flat sincerity. The NJ water quality as a genuine concern elevates the New Jersey joke. Very economical.

jokes.text: Jemaine asks a horse-drawn carriage driver about going to New Jersey; driver says he only rides around the park. Jemaine: 'That's weird. She said she'd done it before.'
jokes.setup: Jemaine is trying to get to New Jersey for his elopement
jokes.punchline: Jemaine expected a Central Park carriage to take him to New Jersey — apparently Keitha claimed this was possible
jokes.explanation: The revelation that Keitha told Jemaine a park carriage could take them to New Jersey — and he believed her — is both funny and quietly reveals Keitha's casual deceptiveness. 'That's weird. She said she'd done it before.' is delivered with complete guilelessness.

jokes.text: Jemaine stops a horse-drawn carriage in Central Park to elope to New Jersey — the driver informs him he only rides around the park
jokes.setup: Jemaine hails a horse-drawn carriage with romantic intentions of eloping
jokes.punchline: The carriage only does park loops — it's a tourist attraction, not transportation — but Keitha had said 'she'd done it before'
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist situational joke — Jemaine trying to elope by tourist carriage is very character-specific. 'She said she'd done it before' as the button implies either Keitha is equally confused or she's been on a previous failed elopement by carriage.

jokes.text: 'Take me to Chinatown and do not delay, sir.' — Jemaine pivoting his elopement plan to a rickshaw
jokes.setup: The carriage can't go to New Jersey; Jemaine redirects
jokes.punchline: 'Take me to Chinatown and do not delay, sir' — the formal, urgent address to a rickshaw driver, and the complete non-sequitur of the destination shift from New Jersey to Chinatown
jokes.explanation: 'Do not delay, sir' — the formal imperative combined with the sudden switch to Chinatown — is perfectly Jemaine. The destination change from New Jersey to Chinatown makes no narrative sense, which is the joke. High character integration; the formality of the address is very specific.

jokes.text: Jemaine, now on the carriage: 'Take me to Chinatown and do not delay, sir.' Driver: 'Just around the park.' Jemaine: 'Ah, yes, you said that. Yes.'
jokes.setup: The carriage driver won't go to New Jersey
jokes.punchline: Jemaine pivots to 'Chinatown, do not delay' — apparently a backup plan — and then meekly accepts 'just around the park' as if remembering the driver's limitations
jokes.explanation: 'Take me to Chinatown and do not delay, sir' delivered with sudden imperious authority, then immediately deflated by 'Ah, yes, you said that. Yes.' — a tiny escalation/collapse structure that works.

jokes.text: 'Look, just around the park.' / 'Ah, yes, you said that.' — the rickshaw also just goes around the park; Jemaine apologies
jokes.setup: Jemaine tries the rickshaw as an alternative to the carriage
jokes.punchline: The rickshaw also only goes around the park — the same limitation — and Jemaine's 'Ah, yes, you said that. Yes.' acknowledges the repetition with weary politeness
jokes.explanation: Good callback — the repeated constraint (everything in the park just goes around the park) is a well-constructed running gag. Jemaine's polite acknowledgment of this fact is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Jemaine returns to find Bret taped to the door. 'Bret, have you seen Keitha?' Bret: 'She robbed us. Her and her friends jumped me and taped me to the door.' Jemaine: 'Did she mention me at all?'
jokes.setup: Jemaine returns to find Bret taped to the door after Keitha and friends robbed them
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's first response to learning of the robbery and assault is to ask whether Keitha mentioned him — his romantic feelings override the crime
jokes.explanation: The punchline is Jemaine's priorities — presented with clear evidence of being victimized, his immediate concern is whether he was in her thoughts during the robbery. 'Did she mention me at all?' is perfectly timed and character-specific. Bret's 'No, sorry, man' is the ideal response.

jokes.text: Bret, still taped to the door: 'Could you pass me my sandwich, please?' — followed by Jemaine's careful sandwich delivery and offer of a napkin
jokes.setup: Bret has been robbed and taped to the door
jokes.punchline: Bret's immediate priority is his sandwich; Jemaine delivers it with domestic care; the offer of a napkin to a man taped to a door is the absurdist peak
jokes.explanation: The sandwich request while taped to the door is wonderfully absurd in its ordinariness. Jemaine's offer of a napkin — Bret's 'No thanks' — and then the hug request that follows creates a lovely sequence of small escalating absurdist beats.

jokes.text: Jemaine: 'Can I get a hug?' Bret: 'No. No.' Jemaine: 'A hug?' — followed by Bret refusing to participate, Jemaine hugging him anyway
jokes.setup: Jemaine is sad about Keitha; Bret is taped to the door
jokes.punchline: Bret's refusal to hug a man he's physically attached to a door — 'I'm not participating in this hug'
jokes.explanation: 'I'm not participating in this hug' while being physically incapable of preventing it is a great line — Bret's dignity under total physical helplessness. The word 'participating' is key: he frames it as a choice even though he has none.

jokes.text: End-credits reprise of 'Too Many Dicks on the Dance Floor' — the song that opened the episode plays again over the credits
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Jemaine back to being single
jokes.punchline: The return to the opening song — the cycle is complete; Jemaine is back at square one, at the nightclub with too many dicks on the dance floor
jokes.explanation: The structural callback of returning to the opening song works as an elegant circular narrative — Jemaine's romantic adventure ended in robbery and heartbreak, and he's back at the beginning. Good formal comedy writing, even if the song itself has already been scored.

tmdb_overview: Jemaine starts dating an Australian, to Bret's dismay and inconvenience.
